title.zh: 卷七十二 王貢兩龔鮑傳
title.en: Volume 72: Wang, Gong, two Gongs and Bao
title.raw: 卷七十二 王貢兩龔鮑傳 Volume 72: Wang, Gong, two Gongs and Bao

sentences.zh: 昔武王伐紂，遷九鼎於雒邑，伯夷、叔齊薄之，餓于首陽，不食其祿，周猶稱盛德焉。

translations.literal: In former times King Wu attacked Zhou, moved the nine tripods to Luoyi; Boyi and Shuqi despised this, starved on Mount Shouyang, and would not eat its salary, yet Zhou was still said to possess abundant virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Long ago, when King Wu overthrew the last king of Shang and had the nine bronze tripods carried to Luoyi, Boyi and Shuqi condemned the new order; they withdrew to Mount Shouyang and starved rather than live off Zhou grain—and still the Zhou could be praised for the moral grandeur it claimed.

sentences.zh: 然孔子賢此二人，以為「不降其志，不辱其身」也。

translations.literal: Yet Confucius deemed these two men worthy, holding that they "did not lower their purpose and did not dishonor their persons."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius nevertheless ranked these two among the worthiest of men, for they had, he said, "refused to bend their resolve and refused to compromise their integrity."

sentences.zh: 而孟子亦云：「聞伯夷之風者，貪夫廉，懦夫有立志；」

translations.literal: And Mencius also said: "Those who hear the air of Boyi, the grasping become incorrupt, the timid establish resolve;"
translations.idiomatic: Mencius likewise observed that whoever feels the moral force of Boyi finds the grasping stirred toward honesty and the faint-hearted stirred toward steadfast purpose—

sentences.zh: 「奮乎百世之上，行乎百世之下莫不興起，非賢人而能若是乎！」

translations.literal: "They rouse themselves above a hundred generations, they move below a hundred generations and none fail to rise—is this possible if one is not a worthy man!"
translations.idiomatic: "—men who stand as models a hundred generations above us and whose example still lifts every generation that follows; who but a true sage could do that?"

sentences.zh: 漢興有園公、綺里季、夏黃公、甪里先生，此四人者，當秦之世，避而入商雒深山，以待天下之定也。

translations.literal: At the rise of Han there were Lord Yuan of the garden, Qili Ji, Lord Xia of Huang, and Master Luli—these four men, in the age of Qin, fled and entered the deep mountains of Shangluo, waiting for the settling of the realm.
translations.idiomatic: When the Han arose, four recluses were remembered—Lord Yuan of the garden, Qili Ji, Lord Xia of Huang, and Master Luli—who, while the Qin still ruled, had withdrawn into the Shangluo range and waited out the turmoil until peace returned.

sentences.zh: 自高祖聞而召之，不至。

translations.literal: From the time Gaozu heard of them and summoned them, they did not come.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Gaozu sent for them once he heard their fame, but they never answered the call.

sentences.zh: 其後呂后用留侯計，使皇太子卑辭束帛致禮，安車迎而致之。

translations.literal: Afterward Empress Lü used the Marquis of Liu's plan, causing the heir apparent to use humble words, silk bundles, and present rites, and a comfortable carriage to welcome them and bring them in.
translations.idiomatic: Later Empress Lü, following Zhang Liang's stratagem, had the crown prince approach them with humble letters, rolls of silk, and full courtesy, and send a cushioned carriage to escort them to court.

sentences.zh: 四人既至，從太子見，高祖客而敬焉，太子得以為重，遂用自安。

translations.literal: When the four men had arrived, they followed the heir apparent to an audience; Gaozu treated them as guests and showed them respect, and the heir apparent thereby gained weight, and so used this to secure himself.
translations.idiomatic: Once they came and attended the heir apparent at audience, Gaozu received them as honored guests; the heir's standing rose at a stroke, and he used their presence to steady his own position.

sentences.zh: 語在留侯傳。

translations.literal: The words are in the biography of the Marquis of Liu.
translations.idiomatic: The full account is given in the biography of Zhang Liang, Marquis of Liu.

sentences.zh: 其後谷口有鄭子真，蜀有嚴君平，皆修身自保，非其服弗服，非其食弗食。

translations.literal: Afterward at Gukou there was Zhen Zizhen of Zheng, and in Shu there was Yan Junping; both cultivated themselves and preserved themselves—what was not their garment they did not wear, what was not their food they did not eat.
translations.idiomatic: Later ages knew Zhen Zizhen of Zheng at Gukou and Yan Junping in Shu—men who guarded their integrity so strictly that they wore only what befitted their station and ate only what their principles allowed.

sentences.zh: 成帝時，元舅大將軍王鳳以禮聘子真，子真遂不詘而終。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Cheng, the chief commander, the Grand General Wang Feng, maternal uncle by the Yuan consort, with rites tried to hire Zizhen; Zizhen finally did not yield and to the end did not go.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Cheng, Grand General Wang Feng—the Yuan consort's brother—sought to engage Zizhen with formal honors; Zizhen refused to the last and never took office.

sentences.zh: 君平卜筮於成都巿，以為「卜筮者賤業，而可以惠眾人。

translations.literal: Junping divined by milfoil and tortoise in the market of Chengdu, holding that "divination is a low trade, yet it can benefit the multitude.
translations.idiomatic: Junping set up his divining stall in the Chengdu market, reasoning that although fortune-telling was a humble occupation, it put him where he could do ordinary people some good.

sentences.zh: 有邪惡非正之問，則依蓍龜為言利害。

translations.literal: When there were questions of evil and not correct, then relying on the milfoil and tortoise he spoke of benefit and harm.
translations.idiomatic: When clients came with crooked or improper questions, he would read the stalks and shell and steer the answer toward what truly helped or harmed them.

sentences.zh: 與人子言依於孝，與人弟言依於順，與人臣言依於忠，各因勢導之以善，從吾言者，已過半矣。」

translations.literal: With those who were sons he spoke relying on filial piety, with those who were younger brothers he spoke relying on obedience, with those who were ministers he spoke relying on loyalty; each according to the situation he guided them toward good, and those who followed his words were already more than half.
translations.idiomatic: To sons he spoke of filial duty, to younger brothers of deference, to officials of loyalty—tailoring counsel to each case until most who listened left the better for it."

sentences.zh: 裁日閱數人，得百錢足自養，則閉肆下簾而授老子。

translations.literal: He limited it to reading a few people per day; when he obtained a hundred cash, enough to support himself, then he closed the shop, lowered the curtain, and taught the Laozi.
translations.idiomatic: He saw only a handful of clients a day; once he had earned the hundred cash he needed for his keep, he shuttered the stall, drew the blind, and turned to expounding the Laozi.

sentences.zh: 博覽亡不通，依老子、嚴周之指著書十餘萬言。

translations.literal: He broadly read and there was nothing he did not penetrate; relying on the pointers of Laozi and Yan Zhou he composed writings of more than a hundred thousand characters.
translations.idiomatic: He read widely and missed nothing essential; guided by Laozi and Zhuang Zhou, he wrote well over a hundred thousand characters of his own.

sentences.zh: 楊雄少時從遊學，以而仕京師顯名，數為朝廷在位賢者稱君平德。

translations.literal: Yang Xiong in his youth studied under him as a traveling student, and thereby became eminent in office in the capital, and several times worthies in office at court praised Junping's virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong had studied with him in his youth before making a name in the capital; more than once high ministers at court repeated praise of Junping's character.

sentences.zh: 杜陵李彊素善雄，久之為益州牧，喜謂雄曰：「吾真得嚴君平矣。」

translations.literal: Li Qiang of Duling was always on good terms with Xiong; after a long time he became Governor of Yi Province, and joyfully said to Xiong, "I have truly obtained Yan Junping."
translations.idiomatic: Li Qiang of Duling, an old friend of Yang Xiong's, was eventually appointed governor of Yi Province and said with delight, "At last I shall meet Yan Junping in person."

sentences.zh: 雄曰：「君備禮以待之，彼人可見而不可得詘也。」

translations.literal: Xiong said, "If you prepare full rites and wait for him, that person can be met but cannot be compelled to yield."
translations.idiomatic: Xiong replied, "Treat him with every courtesy if you like—you may see him, but you will never bend him to your service."

sentences.zh: 彊心以為不然。

translations.literal: Qiang in his heart thought it was not so.
translations.idiomatic: Li Qiang privately dismissed the warning.

sentences.zh: 及至蜀，致禮與相見，卒不敢言以為從事，乃歎曰：「楊子雲誠知人！」

translations.literal: When he reached Shu, he presented rites and met with him, and in the end did not dare speak of making him an attendant, and then sighed, saying, "Yang Ziyun truly knows men!"
translations.idiomatic: In Shu he paid his respects and was received, but he never dared offer Junping a post; afterward he could only marvel, "Yang Ziyun reads character like a mirror."

sentences.zh: 君平年九十餘，遂以其業終，蜀人愛敬，至今稱焉。

translations.literal: Junping lived more than ninety years, and finally ended with that occupation; the people of Shu loved and revered him, and to this day praise him.
translations.idiomatic: Junping lived past ninety and died as he had lived, by the diviner's trade; the people of Shu cherished his memory, and they still speak of him with respect.

sentences.zh: 及雄著書言當世士，稱此二人。

translations.literal: When Xiong composed writings speaking of gentlemen of the age, he praised these two men.
translations.idiomatic: When Yang Xiong later wrote of the leading men of his day, he singled out these two for praise.

sentences.zh: 其論曰：「或問：君子疾沒世而名不稱，盍勢諸？

translations.literal: His discussion says: "Someone asked: The gentleman hates passing from the world without his name being spoken of—why not rely on power?
translations.idiomatic: In his essay he asks: "They say a gentleman dreads leaving no name behind—so why not simply ride influence and office?"

sentences.zh: 名，卿可幾。

translations.literal: Name—can the minister be approached?
translations.idiomatic: "Can reputation really be seized by rank alone?"

sentences.zh: 曰：君子德名為幾。

translations.literal: It was said: The gentleman's virtue and name are what can be approached.
translations.idiomatic: The answer runs: "For the gentleman, moral weight and a true name are what may be drawn near."

sentences.zh: 梁、齊、楚、趙之君非不富且貴也，惡虖成其名！

translations.literal: The lords of Liang, Qi, Chu, and Zhao were not without wealth and honor—how could they complete their names!
translations.idiomatic: The rulers of Liang, Qi, Chu, and Zhao had wealth and rank in abundance—yet none of them won the kind of name that endures.

sentences.zh: 谷口鄭子真不詘其志，耕於巖石之下，名震於京師，豈其卿？

translations.literal: Zhen Zizhen of Gukou did not bend his purpose; he plowed below the cliff rocks; his name shook the capital—is it the minister?
translations.idiomatic: Zhen Zizhen of Gukou never bent his principles; he farmed beneath the cliffs until his reputation shook the capital—was that because he held high office?

sentences.zh: 豈其卿？

translations.literal: Is it the minister?
translations.idiomatic: Was it court rank that did it?

sentences.zh: 楚兩龔之絜，其清矣乎！

translations.literal: The purity of the two Gongs of Chu—how clear it is!
translations.idiomatic: As for the stainless honor of the two Gongs of Chu—could any gloss make it brighter?

sentences.zh: 蜀嚴湛冥，不作苟見，不治苟得，久幽而不改其操，雖隨、和何以加諸？

translations.literal: Yan of Shu is deep and dark; he did not make careless views, did not pursue careless gain; long hidden yet did not change his conduct—though Sui and He, what could they add to him?
translations.idiomatic: Yan of Shu moved in depths beyond easy praise: no opinion offered for effect, no profit taken by compromise; years in obscurity never dulled his standard—what could the fabled jewels of Sui and He add to a man like that?

sentences.zh: 舉茲以旃，不亦寶乎！」

translations.literal: Raising these as a banner—is it not precious!"
translations.idiomatic: To hold such examples before the age—is that not the true treasure?"

sentences.zh: 自園公、綺里季、夏黃公、甪里先生、鄭子真、嚴君平皆未嘗仕，然其風聲足以激貪厲俗，近古之逸民也。

translations.literal: From Lord Yuan of the garden, Qili Ji, Lord Xia of Huang, Master Luli, Zhen Zizhen, and Yan Junping—none ever served in office, yet their reputation was enough to sting the greedy and sharpen custom; they are near to the recluses of antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Yuan, Qili Ji, Lord Xia of Huang, Master Luli, Zhen Zizhen, and Yan Junping never took office, yet the mere rumor of their lives was enough to shame the covetous and lift public morals—men in the spirit of the ancient recluses.

sentences.zh: 若王吉、貢禹、兩龔之屬，皆以禮讓進退云。

translations.literal: As for Wang Ji, Gong Yu, and the sort of the two Gongs, all by ritual yielding advanced and retreated, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji, Gong Yu, and the two Gongs belong to another pattern—men who advanced or withdrew according to ritual propriety, as the text will show.

sentences.zh: 王吉字子陽，琅玡皋虞人也。

translations.literal: Wang Ji, courtesy name Ziyang, was a man of Gaoyu in Langya.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji, style Ziyang, came from Gaoyu in the commandery of Langya.

sentences.zh: 少時學明經，以郡吏舉孝廉為郎，補若盧右丞，遷雲陽令。

translations.literal: In his youth he studied the illumined classics, and by clerk of the commandery was recommended filial and incorrupt and became gentleman; he was appointed right aide of Ruolu, and was transferred to be magistrate of Yunyang.
translations.idiomatic: He mastered the classics in youth, entered the bureaucracy as a commandery clerk, earned recommendation as filial and incorrupt, became a court gentleman, served as right assistant in the Ruolu arsenal, and was promoted magistrate of Yunyang.

sentences.zh: 舉賢良為昌邑中尉，而王好遊獵，驅馳國中，動作亡節，吉上疏諫，曰：

translations.literal: He was recommended worthy and good and became captain of the interior to the king of Changyi, and the king loved roaming and hunting, driving and galloping through the state, his movements without restraint; Ji submitted a memorial remonstrating, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as worthy and good, he was appointed captain of the guard to the king of Changyi—a ruler who loved the chase and tore across his domain without restraint. Wang Ji remonstrated in a memorial that began:

sentences.zh: 臣聞古者師日行三十里，吉行五十里。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that in antiquity on campaign the army went thirty li a day, on good roads fifty li a day.
translations.idiomatic: I have read that armies of old marched thirty li a day on the march and at most fifty when the roads were good.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：「匪風發兮，匪車揭兮，顧瞻周道，中心怛兮。」

translations.literal: The Odes says: "That wind is not swift, that carriage is not raised; I look back on the Zhou road, and my heart is grieved."
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Odes says, "That is no wind of old; it is the wind of haste; that is no carriage of old; it is the carriage lifted at a run; I glance back at the highway of Zhou, and sorrow knots my heart."

sentences.zh: 說曰：是非古之風也，發發者；

translations.literal: The explanation says: That is not the wind of antiquity, the fa-fa one;
translations.idiomatic: The gloss reads: "It is not the measured wind of antiquity—it is the wind of reckless speed."

sentences.zh: 是非古之車也，揭揭者。

translations.literal: that is not the carriage of antiquity, the jie-jie one.
translations.idiomatic: "It is not the steady carriage of antiquity—it is the carriage jolting at a gallop."

sentences.zh: 蓋傷之也。

translations.literal: Probably it wounds this.
translations.idiomatic: The poem is lamenting exactly that kind of recklessness.

sentences.zh: 今者大王幸方與，曾不半日而馳二百里，百姓頗廢耕桑，治道牽馬，臣愚以為民不可數變也。

translations.literal: Now the great king fortunately went to Fangyu, and in not half a day galloped two hundred li; the hundred surnames were much made to abandon plowing and mulberry, repair roads and lead horses; your servant foolishly thinks the people cannot repeatedly be changed.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty lately rode to Fangyu and covered two hundred li before noon; farmers had to drop plow and loom to mend roads and hold horses. I fear the people cannot bear such disruption again and again.

sentences.zh: 昔召公述職，當民事時，舍於棠下而聽斷焉。

translations.literal: In former times the Duke of Shao performed his duties, and when it was the time of the people's affairs, he lodged under the pear tree and listened to judgments.
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Shao, they say, heard lawsuits in the open and even camped under a wild pear so the people could always reach him.

sentences.zh: 是時人皆得其所，後世思其仁恩，至虖不伐甘棠，甘棠之詩是也。

translations.literal: At that time people all obtained their place; later generations thought on his benevolent grace, to the extent of not cutting down the sweet pear; the ode on the sweet pear is this.
translations.idiomatic: Everyone found justice in his day; later ages remembered his kindness so fondly they would not cut the sweet pear where he had sat—the theme of the "Sweet Pear" ode in the Odes.

sentences.zh: 大王不好書術而樂逸游，馮式撙銜，馳騁不止，口倦乎叱吒，手苦於箠轡，身勞乎車輿；

translations.literal: The great king does not love writing and arts but delights in roaming and travel; leaning on the crossbar, gathering the bit, galloping without stop; the mouth tires with shouting, the hand suffers with whip and reins, the body toils in the carriage;
translations.idiomatic: You take little interest in books but great pleasure in the hunt—gripping bar and bit, driving on without pause until voice is raw from shouting, hands raw from whip and rein, and your body shaken inside the carriage.

sentences.zh: 朝則冒霧露，晝則被塵埃，夏則為大暑之所暴炙，冬則為風寒之所匽薄。

translations.literal: In the morning then you brave dew and mist, in the day then you are covered with dust and dirt, in summer then you are violently roasted by the great heat, in winter then you are pressed thin by wind and cold.
translations.idiomatic: Dawn sorties mean mist and dew; daylight rides cloak you in dust; summer sun scorches you; winter wind cuts through you.

sentences.zh: 數以耎脆之玉體犯勤勞之煩毒，非所以全壽命之宗也，又非所以進仁義之隆也。

translations.literal: Repeatedly with a soft and fragile jade body you offend the vexation and poison of toil—this is not the means to preserve the span of life, and again it is not the means to advance the height of benevolence and right.
translations.idiomatic: To strain a body as fine as jade with that kind of exertion is no way to safeguard your years, and no way to deepen the humane and right conduct you owe your people.

sentences.zh: 夫廣夏之下，細旃之上，明師居前，勸誦在後，上論唐虞之際，下及殷周之盛，考仁聖之風，習治國之道，訢訢焉發憤忘食，日新厥德，其樂豈徒銜橛之間哉！

translations.literal: Under a broad summer hall, on fine felt, a bright teacher in front, encouragement to recite in the rear, above discussing the time of Tang and Yu, below reaching the flourishing of Yin and Zhou, examining the air of benevolent sages, studying the way of ordering the state, joyfully forgetting food in issued indignation, daily renewing his virtue—how could the pleasure be only between bit and cheekpiece!
translations.idiomatic: Beneath a broad hall, on fine rugs, with a good teacher before you and readers behind, you could range from the age of Yao and Shun down to the glory of Yin and Zhou, absorb the temper of the sages, and learn how true kings governed—forgetting meals in earnest study and growing better every day. Would that joy be no more than what you find between horse and saddle?

sentences.zh: 休則俛仰詘信以利形，進退步趨以實下，吸新吐故以練臧，專意積精以適神，於以養生，豈不長哉！

translations.literal: In rest then bending and stretching, yielding and extending to benefit the form, advancing and retreating, stepping and hurrying to firm the lower, inhaling the new and exhaling the old to exercise the viscera, concentrating intent and accumulating essence to suit the spirit—in this to nurture life, is it not long!
translations.idiomatic: In quiet hours you could stretch and flex to limber the frame, walk and pace to strengthen the legs, practice breath exercises to clear the inner organs, and gather your spirit to refresh the mind—there is true longevity in that.

sentences.zh: 大王誠留意如此，則心有堯舜之志，體有喬松之壽，美聲廣譽登而上聞，則福祿其轃而社稷安矣。

translations.literal: If the great king sincerely keeps his mind on this, then in mind he will have the purpose of Yao and Shun, in body he will have the span of Qiao and Song; beautiful sound and broad fame will ascend and be heard above, then fortune and salary will follow in tracks and the altars of soil and grain will be secure.
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty would fix your heart on such things, you would match Yao and Shun in intent and Peng Zu and Prince Qiao in years; a good name would reach the throne above you, blessings would follow in train, and the altars of the state would stand firm.

sentences.zh: 皇帝仁聖，至今思慕未怠，於宮館囿池弋獵之樂未有所幸，大王宜夙夜念此，以承聖意。

translations.literal: The emperor is benevolent and sage; to this day his thinking and longing have not slackened; as to the pleasures of palaces, lodges, ponds, and hunting with bow, he has not yet favored them; the great king ought morning and night to think on this, to inherit the sage intention.
translations.idiomatic: The late emperor was humane and wise; the court still mourns him and has not turned to palace pleasures or hunting in the parks. You should weigh that example day and night and show that you share the same mind.

sentences.zh: 諸侯骨肉，莫親大王，大王於屬則子也，於位則臣也，一身而二任之責加焉，恩愛行義孅介有不具者，於以上聞，非饗國之福也。

translations.literal: Among the flesh-and-bone of the feudal lords none is closer than the great king; the great king in relationship is a son, in position is a minister; on one body the responsibility of two posts is added; if in loving kindness and conduct of right there is something not complete, and from this it is heard above, it is not the fortune of the state that receives offerings.
translations.idiomatic: Of all imperial kinsmen none stands closer to the throne than you: you are a son by blood yet a subject by rank, and both ties bind you at once. Any lapse in love or duty that reaches the capital bodes ill for the whole realm.

sentences.zh: 臣吉愚戇，願大王察之。

translations.literal: Your servant Ji is foolish and blunt; I wish the great king to examine it.
translations.idiomatic: I speak bluntly, Your Majesty; I beg you to weigh these words.

sentences.zh: 王賀雖不遵道，然猶知敬禮吉，乃下令曰：「寡人造行不能無惰，中慰甚忠，數輔吾過。

translations.literal: King He although did not follow the way, yet still knew to respect and treat with ritual Ji, and thereupon issued an order saying: "The solitary man's conduct cannot be without laziness; the captain of the interior is very loyal and repeatedly assists my faults.
translations.idiomatic: King He paid little heed to duty, yet he still respected Wang Ji; he issued an edict saying, "I cannot claim perfect conduct, but my captain of the interior has served me with utter loyalty and has again and again corrected my faults.

sentences.zh: 使謁者千秋賜中尉牛肉五百斤，酒五石，脯五束。」

translations.literal: Let the usher Qianqiu present to the captain of the interior five hundred jin of beef, five shi of wine, five bundles of dried meat."
translations.idiomatic: Let usher Qianqiu convey to him five hundred jin of beef, five shi of wine, and five bundles of dried meat as my gift."

sentences.zh: 其後復放從自若。

translations.literal: Afterward again he released himself and followed his own way as before.
translations.idiomatic: After that he went back to his old habits as if nothing had been said.

sentences.zh: 吉輒諫爭，甚得輔弼之義，雖不治民，國中莫不敬重焉。

translations.literal: Ji repeatedly remonstrated and disputed, greatly obtaining the meaning of assisting and correcting; although he did not govern the people, in the state none did not respect and honor him.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji kept arguing against the misconduct and fulfilled the true office of a remonstrator; though he held no civil portfolio, everyone in the kingdom respected him.

sentences.zh: 久之，昭帝崩，亡嗣，大將軍霍光秉政，遣大鴻臚宗正迎昌邑王。

translations.literal: After a long time Emperor Zhao died, without heir; the Grand General Huo Guang held power, and sent the Grand Herald and the Director of the Imperial Clan to welcome the king of Changyi.
translations.idiomatic: Years later, when Emperor Zhao died without an heir, Huo Guang as regent dispatched the Grand Herald and the imperial-clan director to escort the king of Changyi to the capital.

sentences.zh: 吉即奏書戒王曰：「臣聞高宗諒闇，三年不言。

translations.literal: Ji immediately submitted a memorial warning the king, saying: "Your servant has heard that Gaozong dwelt in the mourner's hut and for three years did not speak.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji at once sent a sealed warning: "I have read that King Gaozong of Shang observed deep mourning and for three years scarcely spoke.

sentences.zh: 今大王以喪事徵，宜日夜哭泣悲哀而已，慎毋有所發。

translations.literal: Now the great king is summoned on account of mourning affairs; you ought day and night to weep and be sorrowful only, carefully do not have anything to issue.
translations.idiomatic: You have been called to the capital for a funeral; your whole demeanor should be tears and grief, and you must issue no rash orders.

sentences.zh: 且何獨喪事，凡南面之君何言哉？

translations.literal: Moreover how only mourning affairs—generally for a lord facing south what words are there?
translations.idiomatic: And not for mourning alone: what business has a ruler who faces south to chatter about?

sentences.zh: 天不言，四時行焉，百物生焉，願大王察之。

translations.literal: Heaven does not speak, yet the four seasons proceed; the hundred things are born; I wish the great king to examine this.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven is silent, yet the seasons turn and the myriad things grow—consider what that means for a Son of Heaven."

sentences.zh: 大將軍仁愛勇智，忠信之德天下莫不聞，事孝武皇帝二十餘年未嘗有過。

translations.literal: The Grand General is benevolent, loving, brave, and wise; the virtue of loyalty and trustworthiness—none under Heaven have not heard; serving Emperor Wu more than twenty years, he never had fault.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand General combines humanity, courage, and wisdom, and his name for loyalty is known everywhere; he served Emperor Wu for more than twenty years without a stain.

sentences.zh: 先帝棄群臣，屬以天下，寄幼孤焉，大將軍抱持幼君襁褓之中，布政施教，海內晏然，雖周公、伊尹亡以加也。

translations.literal: The former emperor abandoned the host of ministers, entrusted to him the realm, and lodged the young orphan with him; the Grand General embraced and held the young lord in swaddling clothes, spread government and taught transformation, and within the seas there was peace; though the Duke of Zhou and Yi Yin, there was nothing to add.
translations.idiomatic: The late emperor left the empire in his hands and the infant heir in his arms; he has governed from the cradle and kept the realm at peace—work that not even the Duke of Zhou or Yi Yin could surpass.

sentences.zh: 今帝崩亡嗣，大將軍惟思可以奉宗廟者，攀援而立大王，其仁厚豈有量哉！

translations.literal: Now the emperor has died without heir; the Grand General has thought only of who can serve the ancestral temple, climbed and pulled and established the great king—how can his benevolence and thickness be measured!
translations.idiomatic: Now that the throne is empty, he has searched the bloodline and raised you to it—kindness on that scale cannot be reckoned.

sentences.zh: 臣願大王事之敬之，政事壹聽之，大王垂拱南面而已。

translations.literal: Your servant wishes the great king to serve him and respect him, government affairs uniformly listen to him; the great king hangs his sash and faces south—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Serve him with deference, yield every decision of state to him, and content yourself with the empty dignity of the throne—that is how you will survive this moment.

sentences.zh: 願留意，嘗以為念。」

translations.literal: I wish you to keep your mind on it; always take it as thought."
translations.idiomatic: Fix these words in your mind and never forget them."

sentences.zh: 王既到，即位二十餘日以行淫亂廢。

translations.literal: The king having arrived, after more than twenty days on the throne was deposed for conduct of licentious disorder.
translations.idiomatic: The new sovereign reached the capital and within a few weeks was deposed for debauchery and disorder.

sentences.zh: 昌邑群臣坐在國時不舉奏王罪過，令漢朝不聞知，又不能輔道，陷王大惡，皆下獄誅。

translations.literal: The host of ministers of Changyi, sitting for when in the state they did not raise and memorialize the king's faults and crimes, causing the Han court not to hear and know, and again being unable to assist and guide, trapping the king in great evil, all were cast into prison and executed.
translations.idiomatic: His Changyi attendants, who had stayed silent about his crimes at home and failed the court with their counsel until he fell into utter disgrace, were arrested and put to death.

sentences.zh: 唯吉與郎中令龔遂以忠直數諫正得減死，髡為城旦。

translations.literal: Only Ji and the Director of the Gentlemen of the Palace, Gong Sui, by loyalty and uprightness repeatedly remonstrated and corrected, obtained reduction of death, and were shaved for wall dawn.
translations.idiomatic: Only Wang Ji and Gong Sui, captain of the palace gentlemen, escaped execution because they had remonstrated faithfully; their sentence was commuted to shaved-head corvée labor.

sentences.zh: 起家復為益州刺史，病去官，復徵為博士諫大夫。

translations.literal: Rising from home he again became Governor of Yi Province, by illness left office, and again was summoned to be erudite and remonstrance grandee.
translations.idiomatic: He was later recalled from private life as governor of Yi Province, retired on grounds of ill health, then summoned back as an erudite and remonstrance grandee.

sentences.zh: 是時宣帝頗修武帝故事，宮室車服盛於昭帝。

translations.literal: At this time Emperor Xuan largely cultivated the precedents of Emperor Wu; palaces, chambers, chariots, and garments exceeded those of Emperor Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan was reviving Emperor Wu's scale of splendor: palaces, wardrobes, and equipage soon outshone anything seen under Emperor Zhao.

sentences.zh: 時外戚許、史、王氏貴寵，而上躬親政事，任用能吏。

translations.literal: At that time the outer affines Xu, Shi, and Wang were noble and favored, yet the emperor personally attended to government affairs and employed able clerks.
translations.idiomatic: The Xu, Shi, and Wang affines by marriage dominated the inner court, but the emperor himself presided over policy and appointed capable administrators.

sentences.zh: 吉上疏言得失，曰：

translations.literal: Ji submitted a memorial speaking of gain and loss, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji therefore memorialized on what the court was doing right and wrong:

sentences.zh: 陛下躬聖質，總萬方，帝王圖籍日陳于前，惟思世務，將興太平。

translations.literal: Your Majesty personally possesses sage substance, gathers the myriad regions, imperial and kingly charts and registers daily are displayed before you; only thinking of the affairs of the age, you will raise great peace.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty bears a sage-like nature and holds the realm in hand; maps and registers lie open before you daily while you ponder the business of the age and seek to bring about great peace.

sentences.zh: 詔書每下，民欣然若更生。

translations.literal: Each time an edict descends, the people joyfully are as if reborn.
translations.idiomatic: Every edict from your desk leaves the people feeling as though they had been given new life.

sentences.zh: 臣伏而思之，可謂至恩，未可謂本務也。

translations.literal: Your servant has bowed and thought on it; it can be called utmost favor, yet it cannot be called the root task.
translations.idiomatic: With respect, that is supreme benevolence—but benevolence alone is not yet the root of good order.

sentences.zh: 欲治之主不世出，公卿幸得遭遇其時，言聽諫從，然未有建萬世之長策，舉明主於三代之隆者也。

translations.literal: Rulers who desire order do not emerge every generation; the dukes and ministers fortunately have encountered this time, words are heard and remonstrance is followed, yet there has not been establishing the long strategy of ten thousand generations, raising the bright lord to the height of the Three Ages.
translations.idiomatic: Capable sovereigns are rare; ministers who live under one should seize the chance. You are heard when you speak, yet no one has framed a policy for the ages or lifted this reign to the level of the golden Three Dynasties.

sentences.zh: 其務在於期會簿書，斷獄聽訟而已，此非太平之基也。

translations.literal: Their task lies in deadlines and account books, cutting cases and listening to lawsuits—that is all; this is not the foundation of great peace.
translations.idiomatic: Officialdom busies itself with deadlines, ledgers, and lawsuits; that is no foundation for lasting tranquillity.

sentences.zh: 臣聞聖王宣德流化，必自近始。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the sage king spreads virtue and flowing transformation must begin from what is near.
translations.idiomatic: I have been taught that when a sage king wants his virtue to transform the realm, he begins with what is closest to him.

sentences.zh: 朝廷不備，難以言治；

translations.literal: If the court is not prepared, it is difficult to speak of order;
translations.idiomatic: If the court itself is not in order, you cannot speak of governing the world;

sentences.zh: 左右不正，難以化遠。

translations.literal: if those at the right and left are not correct, it is difficult to transform what is distant.
translations.idiomatic: if those who attend you are not upright, you cannot hope to reform the farthest village.

sentences.zh: 民者，弱而不可勝，愚而不可欺也。

translations.literal: The people are weak yet cannot be overcome, foolish yet cannot be deceived.
translations.idiomatic: The common people may seem powerless, yet they cannot be crushed; they may seem simple, yet they cannot be tricked forever.

sentences.zh: 聖主獨行於深宮，得則天下稱誦之，失則天下咸言之。

translations.literal: The sage lord alone walks in the deep palace; if he obtains, then all under Heaven praise and recite him; if he loses, then all under Heaven together speak of him.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven moves behind high walls: do right and the empire sings your praise; slip once and every lane carries the tale.

sentences.zh: 行發於近，必見於遠，故謹選左右，審擇所使；

translations.literal: Conduct issued from what is near must appear in what is distant; therefore carefully select the right and left, examine and choose those sent;
translations.idiomatic: What begins beside the throne shows itself on the frontier; choose companions and envoys with care.

sentences.zh: 左右所以正身也，所使所以宣德也。

translations.literal: the right and left are the means to correct the body; those sent are the means to spread virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Those at your elbow set the standard for your own conduct; those you dispatch carry your virtue abroad.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：「濟濟多士，文王以寧。」

translations.literal: The Odes says: "Many are the officers, and by them King Wen enjoys tranquillity."
translations.idiomatic: As the Odes put it, "In hosts of good officers King Wen found his peace."

sentences.zh: 此其本也。

translations.literal: This is its root.
translations.idiomatic: That is the root of the matter.

sentences.zh: 春秋所以大一統者，六合同風，九州共貫也。

translations.literal: That for which the Spring and Autumn Annals unify the great unity: the six directions share one wind, the nine provinces share one cord.
translations.idiomatic: The Annals praise "great unity"—one moral climate across the six directions, one political thread binding the nine provinces.

sentences.zh: 今俗吏所以牧民者，非有禮義科指可世世通行者也，獨設刑法以守之。

translations.literal: Now the vulgar clerks who thereby shepherd the people do not have ritual and right, principles and guidelines that can be passed generation after generation; alone they set up punishments and laws to guard them.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the clerks who govern the people today have no ritual framework that could hold for generations; they rely on statutes and penalties alone.

sentences.zh: 其欲治者，不知所繇，以意穿鑿，各取一切，權譎自在，故一變之後不可復修也。

translations.literal: Those who desire order do not know whereby; boring according to intent, each takes one-sided measures, power and craft are self-existent; therefore after one change it cannot again be repaired.
translations.idiomatic: Men who want order grope without a map, improvise from whim, grab stopgap powers, and leave the system so twisted that each "reform" only makes the next harder.

sentences.zh: 是以百里不同風，千里不同俗，戶異政，人殊服，詐偽萌生，刑罰亡極，質樸日銷，恩愛寖薄。

translations.literal: Therefore within a hundred li the winds are not the same, within a thousand li the customs are not the same, households differ in policy, people differ in dress, deceit and falsehood sprout and grow, punishments and penalties are without limit, plain substance daily melts away, loving kindness gradually thins.
translations.idiomatic: The result is a hundred different local habits, a thousand petty policies, fraud on every side, punishments without end, plain folk manners eroded, and good will drained away.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰「安上治民，莫善於禮」，非空言也。

translations.literal: Confucius said, "To settle superiors and order the people, nothing is better than ritual"—it is not empty words.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "For securing ruler and people, nothing matches ritual"—that was no idle slogan.

sentences.zh: 王者未制禮之時，引先王禮宜於今者而用之。

translations.literal: When the king who possesses the state has not yet instituted ritual, he cites the ritual of former kings that fits the present and uses it.
translations.idiomatic: Until a true king can promulgate new rites, he should revive whatever in the old kings' codes still fits the times.

sentences.zh: 臣願陛下承天心，發大業，與公卿大臣延及儒生，述舊禮，明王制，驅一世之民濟之仁壽之域，則俗何以不若成康，壽何以不若高宗？

translations.literal: Your servant wishes Your Majesty to inherit Heaven's mind, launch the great enterprise, together with dukes, ministers, great men, extend to the Confucian scholars, narrate old ritual, clarify kingly institutions, drive the people of one age into the field of benevolence and long life—then how could customs not be like Cheng and Kang, how could longevity not be like the High Ancestor?
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to heed Heaven, undertake the great work, call in high ministers and scholars, recover the classical rites, clarify the institutions of true kingship, and lead this generation into the realm of benevolence and long life—then our age could rival Cheng and Kang, and your own years those of King Wu.

sentences.zh: 竊見當世趨務不合於道者，謹條奏，唯陛下財擇焉。

translations.literal: Your servant privately sees the pursuits of the present age not agreeing with the Way; respectfully I list them in a memorial; only may Your Majesty measure and choose.
translations.idiomatic: I have noted several ways in which current fashion departs from the Way; I list them below for your judgment.

sentences.zh: 吉意以為「夫婦，人倫大綱，夭壽之萌也。

translations.literal: Ji's idea held that "husband and wife are the great norm of human relations, the sprout of early death and long life.
translations.idiomatic: Among Wang Ji's points was this: marriage is the great bond of human society and the first cause of long life or early death.

sentences.zh: 世俗嫁娶太早，未知為人父母之道而有子，是以教化不明而民多夭。

translations.literal: The vulgar world marries too early, not yet knowing the way of being father and mother yet having children; therefore transformation is not clear and the people mostly die early.
translations.idiomatic: People marry too young: they raise children before they understand parenthood, so moral instruction fails and many die before their time.

sentences.zh: 聘妻送女亡節，則貧人不及，故不舉子。

translations.literal: Betrothed wife sending off the daughter lacks restraint, then the poor cannot keep up, therefore they do not raise children.
translations.idiomatic: Betrothal gifts and wedding costs know no limit, so the poor cannot afford to marry and forgo having children altogether.

sentences.zh: 又漢家列侯尚公主，諸侯則國人承翁主，使男事女，夫詘於婦，逆陰陽之位，故多女亂。

translations.literal: Moreover the Han house: full marquises take imperial princesses in marriage; the feudal lords then men of the state take princesses of lords (wengzhu), causing men to serve women, husbands bent under wives, reversing the positions of yin and yang; therefore there is much female disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, Han usage joins full marquises to imperial princesses and pairs the heirs of kings with princesses of the imperial house; the husband becomes the wife's subordinate, yin and yang trade places, and women's factional strife spreads through the court.

sentences.zh: 古者衣服車馬貴賤有章，以褒有德而別尊卑，今上下僭差，人人自制，是以貪財趨利，不畏死亡。

translations.literal: In antiquity for garments, chariots, and horses noble and base had regulations, thereby to praise those with virtue and distinguish high and low; now high and low transgress difference, each person makes his own; therefore they covet wealth and rush profit, and do not fear death.
translations.idiomatic: Antiquity regulated dress and equipage by rank so virtue was honored and hierarchy clear; today everyone tailors his own display, which drives the scramble for wealth without thought for life or law.

sentences.zh: 周之所以能致治，刑措而不用者，以其禁邪於冥冥，絕惡於未萌也。」

translations.literal: That whereby Zhou was able to arrive at order, punishments were set aside and not used, is because it forbade wrong in the dark, cut off evil before it sprouted."
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou achieved order with the rack nearly idle because they checked vice while it was still hidden and stopped crime before it could sprout."

sentences.zh: 又言「舜、湯不用三公九卿之世而舉皋陶、伊尹，不仁者遠。

translations.literal: Again he said, "Shun and Tang did not use the generations of the Three Dukes and nine ministers yet raised Gao Yao and Yi Yin; the not benevolent were distant.
translations.idiomatic: He added, "Shun and Tang did not promote men merely because their fathers had been ministers; they lifted Gao Yao and Yi Yin on merit, and the unworthy shrank away.

sentences.zh: 今使俗吏得任子弟，率多驕驁，不通古今，至於積功治人，亡益於民，此伐檀所為作也。

translations.literal: Now causing vulgar clerks to obtain appointment of sons and brothers, mostly they are proud and untamed, do not penetrate antiquity and the present; as for accumulating merit and ordering people, there is no benefit to the people—this is why "Cutting Sandalwood" was made."
translations.idiomatic: Today clerks who inherit office through their sons are often arrogant ignoramuses who know nothing of past or present; they neither build real achievement nor help the people—that is the evil the Odes lament in "Hewing the sandalwood."

sentences.zh: 宜明選求賢，除任子之令。

translations.literal: It is fitting to clarify selection and seek the worthy, and remove the ordinance of appointing sons.
translations.idiomatic: Open appointments to true talent and abolish the hereditary privilege of "sons of officials."

sentences.zh: 外家及故人可厚以財，不宜居位。

translations.literal: Maternal relatives and old friends can be thickly given wealth; it is not fitting that they occupy positions.
translations.idiomatic: Reward in-laws and old friends with riches if you wish, but do not park them in office.

sentences.zh: 去角抵，減樂府，省尚方，明視天下以儉。

translations.literal: Remove horn butting, reduce the Music Bureau, lessen the Imperial Workshop—make clear to the realm by frugality.
translations.idiomatic: Ban wrestling spectacles, shrink the court music office, cut the palace workshops—and show the empire that you mean to live plainly.

sentences.zh: 古者工不造琱瑑，商不通侈靡，非工商之獨賢，政教使之然也。

translations.literal: In antiquity craftsmen did not make carved and inlaid work, merchants did not traffic in extravagance—not that craftsmen and merchants alone were worthy; government and teaching made them thus."
translations.idiomatic: In a well-ordered age artisans did not turn out useless luxuries and merchants did not deal in excess—not because those classes were morally better, but because policy shaped them."

sentences.zh: 民見儉則歸本，本立而末成。」

translations.literal: When the people see frugality then they return to root; root is established and branch tips are completed."
translations.idiomatic: When the people see thrift at court, they return to the fundamentals; once the root is firm, the whole tree of society can flourish."

sentences.zh: 其指如此，上以其言迂闊，不甚寵異也。

translations.literal: His pointers were like this; the emperor because his words were vast and impractical did not greatly favor or treat him as special.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor judged his advice too lofty for practice and gave him no special favor.

sentences.zh: 吉遂謝病歸琅邪。

translations.literal: Ji thereupon declined illness and returned to Langya.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji pleaded illness and retired to Langya.

sentences.zh: 始吉少時學問，居長安。

translations.literal: At first when Ji was young he studied and questioned, dwelling in Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: In his youth, while studying in Chang'an, Wang Ji lodged there for years.

sentences.zh: 東家有大棗樹垂吉庭中，吉婦取棗以啖吉。

translations.literal: The east neighbor had a large jujube tree that hung into Ji's courtyard; Ji's wife took jujubes to feed Ji.
translations.idiomatic: A neighbor's jujube bough overhung his yard; his wife picked the fruit to serve him.

sentences.zh: 吉後知之，乃去婦。

translations.literal: Ji afterward learned of it, then dismissed the wife.
translations.idiomatic: When he learned she had taken another man's fruit without permission, he divorced her.

sentences.zh: 東家聞而欲伐其樹，鄰里共止之，因固請吉令還婦。

translations.literal: The east neighbor heard and wished to cut down its tree; the neighborhood together stopped them, and therefore firmly asked Ji to order the return of the wife.
translations.idiomatic: The neighbor was so ashamed that he meant to fell the tree; the lane-mates stopped him and begged Wang Ji to take his wife back.

sentences.zh: 里中為之語曰：「東家有樹，王陽婦去；

translations.literal: In the village for this there was a saying: "The east neighbor has a tree; Wang Yang's wife departs;
translations.idiomatic: A rhyme ran through the ward: "The east side had a tree—Wang Yang's wife had to flee;

sentences.zh: 東家棗完，去婦復還。」

translations.literal: the east neighbor's jujubes are complete, the dismissed wife again returns."
translations.idiomatic: the east side spared its fruit—and Wang Yang's wife came home again."

sentences.zh: 其厲志如此。

translations.literal: His sharpening of purpose was like this.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the rigor of his self-discipline.

sentences.zh: 吉與貢禹為友，世稱「王陽在位，貢公彈冠」，言其取舍同也。

translations.literal: Ji and Gong Yu were friends; the age called it "When Wang Yang holds office, Gong dusts his cap," speaking of their taking and leaving being the same.
translations.idiomatic: He and Gong Yu were close friends, and contemporaries said, "When Wang Yang takes a post, Gong Yu brushes off his official cap"—meaning they rose or withdrew in moral unison.

sentences.zh: 元帝初即位，遣使者徵貢禹與吉。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan first ascended the throne, he sent envoys to summon Gong Yu and Ji.
translations.idiomatic: At the beginning of Emperor Yuan's reign the court summoned both Gong Yu and Wang Ji.

sentences.zh: 吉年老，道病卒，上悼之，復遣使者弔祠云。

translations.literal: Ji was old in years, on the road by illness died; the emperor mourned him, and again sent envoys to offer condolences and sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji was elderly and died en route from illness; the emperor grieved and sent envoys to honor him with mourning rites.

sentences.zh: 初，吉兼通五經，能為騶氏春秋，以詩、論語教授，好梁丘賀說易，令子駿受焉。

translations.literal: At first Ji comprehensively penetrated the Five Classics, was able to do the Spring and Autumn of the Zou clan, by the Odes and Analects taught; he loved Liangqiu He's exposition of the Changes, and ordered his son Jun to receive it.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji had mastered the Five Classics and lectured on the Zou version of the Spring and Autumn and on the Odes and Analects; he admired Liangqiu He's reading of the Book of Changes and had his son Wang Jun study under that tradition.

sentences.zh: 駿以孝廉為郎。

translations.literal: Jun by filial and incorrupt became gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Jun entered the court as a gentleman on a filial-and-incorrupt recommendation.

sentences.zh: 左曹陳咸薦駿賢父子，經明行修，宜顯以厲俗。

translations.literal: Left aide Chen Xian recommended Jun worthy father and son, classics illumined conduct cultivated, it is fitting to display them to sharpen custom.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xian of the Left Aides memorialized that father and son were both worthy—learned in the classics and impeccable in conduct—and deserved high visibility as a model for the realm.

sentences.zh: 光祿勳匡衡亦舉駿有專對材。

translations.literal: Superintendent of the Imperial Household Kuang Heng also raised Jun as having talent for exclusive response.
translations.idiomatic: Kuang Heng, superintendent of the imperial household, likewise recommended Wang Jun for his skill in debate and policy.

sentences.zh: 遷諫大夫，使責淮陽憲王。

translations.literal: He was transferred remonstrance grandee, was sent to reprove the king of Huaiyang, Xian.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to remonstrance grandee and dispatched to admonish King Xian of Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 遷趙內史。

translations.literal: He was transferred to be Administrator of the Interior for Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: He was then named interior administrator of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 吉坐昌邑王被刑後，戒子孫毋為王國吏，故駿道病，免官歸。

translations.literal: Because Ji sat for the king of Changyi having received punishment afterward, he warned sons and grandsons not to be clerks of royal states; therefore Jun on the road by illness left office and returned.
translations.idiomatic: After Wang Ji's brush with execution over the king of Changyi, he forbade his descendants to serve feudal courts; Wang Jun therefore pleaded illness, resigned his Zhao post, and went home.

sentences.zh: 起家復為幽州刺史，遷司隸校尉，奏免丞相匡衡，遷少府。

translations.literal: Rising from home he again became Governor of You Province, was transferred Metropolitan Superintendent, submitted a memorial and dismissed Chancellor Kuang Heng, was transferred Superintendent of the Lesser Treasury.
translations.idiomatic: Recalled from retirement, he served as governor of You, then as metropolitan superintendent—where he secured the dismissal of Chancellor Kuang Heng—before moving to superintendent of the lesser treasury.

sentences.zh: 八歲，成帝欲大用之，出駿為京兆尹，試以政事。

translations.literal: In the eighth year, Emperor Cheng wished greatly to use him, sent Jun out as Metropolitan Governor, and tested him with government affairs.
translations.idiomatic: In the eighth year of Emperor Cheng's reign the throne meant to give him heavier duties, named him metropolitan governor of the capital, and watched how he managed administrative business.

sentences.zh: 先是京兆有趙廣漢、張敞、王尊、王章，至駿皆有能名，故京師稱曰：「前有趙、張，後有三王。」

translations.literal: Before this the capital district had Zhao Guanghan, Zhang Chang, Wang Zun, and Wang Zhang; down to Jun all had a reputation for ability; therefore the capital called it: "Before there were Zhao and Zhang; after there are three Wangs."
translations.idiomatic: The metropolitan seat had once been governed by the likes of Zhao Guanghan, Zhang Chang, Wang Zun, and Wang Zhang; Wang Jun upheld the same reputation, so the capital saying ran, "First the two of Zhao and Zhang, then the three Wangs."

sentences.zh: 而薛宣從左馮翊代駿為少府，會御史大夫缺，谷永奏言：「聖王不以名譽加於實效。

translations.literal: And Xue Yan from Left Fengyi replaced Jun as Superintendent of the Lesser Treasury; it happened the imperial counselor was vacant; Gu Yong submitted words saying: "The sage king does not add reputation to actual effect.
translations.idiomatic: When Xue Yan left his post as governor of Left Fengyi to succeed Wang Jun as lesser-treasury superintendent, the office of imperial counselor fell vacant. Gu Yong argued, "A wise ruler does not hand out titles ahead of proven results.

sentences.zh: 考績用人之法，薛宣政事已試。」

translations.literal: The method of examining merit and employing people—Xue Yan's government affairs have already been tested."
translations.idiomatic: Xue Yan's administrative record is already on the books—use that as your measure of merit."

sentences.zh: 上然其議。

translations.literal: The emperor approved his discussion.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted the recommendation.

sentences.zh: 宣為少府月餘，遂超御史大夫，至丞相。

translations.literal: Yan as Superintendent of the Lesser Treasury a bit more than a month, then leaped to imperial counselor, reaching chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Within a month Xue Yan jumped from lesser treasury to imperial counselor and soon reached the chancellorship.

sentences.zh: 駿乃代宣為御史大夫，並居位。

translations.literal: Jun then replaced Yan as imperial counselor, and together they held position.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Jun stepped into Xue Yan's former post as imperial counselor while both men still held top rank.

sentences.zh: 六歲病卒，翟方進代駿為大夫。

translations.literal: Six years he by illness died; Zhai Fangjin replaced Jun as grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Six years later Wang Jun died in office; Zhai Fangjin succeeded him as imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 數月，薛宣免，遂代為丞相。

translations.literal: Several months, Xue Yan was dismissed, then replaced as chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Months afterward Xue Yan was removed, and Zhai Fangjin became chancellor in his place.

sentences.zh: 眾人為駿恨不得封侯。

translations.literal: The host of people for Jun regretted that he could not obtain enfeoffment as marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Public opinion held it a shame that Wang Jun never received a marquisate.

sentences.zh: 駿為少府時，妻死，因不復娶，或問之，駿曰：「德非曾參，子非華、元，亦何敢娶？」

translations.literal: When Jun was Superintendent of the Lesser Treasury, his wife died, and therefore he did not again marry; someone asked him; Jun said, "Virtue is not Zeng Shen's, sons are not Hua and Yuan—how dare I again marry?"
translations.idiomatic: After his wife died while he was lesser-treasury superintendent he refused remarriage; when asked why, he answered, "I am no Zeng Shen in virtue, nor are my sons like Zeng Hua and Zeng Yuan—what right have I to take another wife?"

sentences.zh: 自吉至崇，世名清廉，然材器名稱稍不能及父，而祿位彌隆。

translations.literal: From Ji down to Chong, generations were named pure and incorrupt, yet talent and vessel and name and title gradually could not reach the father, while salary and position became more lofty.
translations.idiomatic: From Wang Ji to his descendant Wang Chong the family kept its name for integrity, though later men never quite matched the founder's stature even as their offices grew grander.

sentences.zh: 皆好車馬衣服，其自奉養極為鮮明，而亡金銀錦繡之物。

translations.literal: All loved chariots, horses, and garments; their self-supporting nurture was extremely bright and fresh, yet there were no objects of gold, silver, brocade, or embroidery.
translations.idiomatic: They enjoyed fine horses and handsome dress and lived in conspicuous comfort, yet they kept no hoard of gold, silver, or brocaded silks.

sentences.zh: 及遷徙去處，所載不過囊衣，不畜積餘財。

translations.literal: When moving residence and going place, what was loaded did not exceed bag garments; they did not store accumulated surplus wealth.
translations.idiomatic: When they moved house their baggage was no more than a few sacks of clothing; they laid up no surplus wealth.

sentences.zh: 去位家居，亦布衣疏食。

translations.literal: Leaving position and dwelling at home, likewise plain cloth and coarse food.
translations.idiomatic: Out of office they wore homespun and ate simply.

sentences.zh: 天下服其廉而怪其奢，故俗傳「王陽能作黃金」。

translations.literal: All under Heaven submitted to their purity yet marveled at their extravagance; therefore vulgar tradition transmitted "Wang Yang was able to make yellow gold."
translations.idiomatic: The empire admired their honesty yet wondered at their lavish style, so rumor invented the tale that "Wang Yang knew how to mint gold."

sentences.zh: 貢禹字少翁，琅邪人也。

translations.literal: Gong Yu, courtesy name Shao'weng, was a man of Langya.
translations.idiomatic: Gong Yu, style Shao'weng, came from Langya commandery.

sentences.zh: 以明經絜行著聞，徵為博士，涼州刺史，病去官。

translations.literal: By illumined classics and pure conduct he was renowned and heard; he was summoned to be erudite, Governor of Liang Province, by illness left office.
translations.idiomatic: His mastery of the classics and spotless conduct won him fame; the court summoned him as an erudite and as governor of Liang Province, but ill health forced him to retire.

sentences.zh: 復舉賢良為河南令。

translations.literal: Again he was recommended worthy and good and became magistrate of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended again as worthy and good, he became magistrate of Henan.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，以職事為府官所責，免冠謝。

translations.literal: After more than a year, because of official duties he was blamed by a superior official, removed cap and apologized.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a year later a superior censured him over routine paperwork; he took off his cap to apologize.

sentences.zh: 禹曰：「冠壹免，安復可冠也！」

translations.literal: Yu said: "Once the cap is removed, how can it again be capped!"
translations.idiomatic: Gong Yu replied, "If the court has stripped me of my cap, it will never fit again."

sentences.zh: 遂去官。

translations.literal: Thereupon he left office.
translations.idiomatic: He resigned on the spot.

sentences.zh: 元帝初即位，徵禹為諫大夫，數虛己問以政事。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan first ascended the throne, he summoned Yu to be remonstrance grandee, several times emptied himself and asked with regard to government affairs.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Yuan came to the throne he called Gong Yu back as remonstrance grandee and repeatedly set aside rank to ask his counsel on state affairs.

sentences.zh: 是時年歲不登，郡國多困，禹奏言：

translations.literal: At this time years did not ascend; commanderies and states were many in distress; Yu submitted words saying:
translations.idiomatic: Harvests failed that year and commanderies were in distress; Gong Yu therefore memorialized:

sentences.zh: 古者宮室有制，宮女不過九人，秣馬不過八匹；

translations.literal: In antiquity palaces and chambers had regulation; palace women did not exceed nine persons, fodder horses did not exceed eight pairs;
translations.idiomatic: Antiquity fixed the scale of the palace: no more than nine ladies-in-waiting and eight teams of chariot horses.

sentences.zh: 牆塗而不琱，木摩而不刻，車輿器物皆不文畫，苑囿不過數十里，與民共之；

translations.literal: walls smeared but not carved, wood rubbed but not engraved, chariots and utensils all were not patterned or painted, parks and preserves did not exceed several tens of li, and were shared with the people;
translations.idiomatic: Walls were whitewashed, not carved; timber was planed smooth, not inlaid; carriages and vessels carried no gilded designs; royal parks ran only a few dozen li and commoners could enter them.

sentences.zh: 任賢使能，什一而稅，亡它賦斂繇戍之役，使民歲不過三日，千里之內自給，千里之外各置貢職而已。

translations.literal: They employed the worthy and used the able, one-tenth taxation, no other levies, corvée, or garrison duties, causing the people's years not to exceed three days, within a thousand li self-supplying, beyond a thousand li each setting tribute duties only.
translations.idiomatic: Rulers appointed talent, took a tenth in tax, imposed no extra levies or corvée beyond three days a year, kept supplies local within a thousand li, and required only standard tribute from farther regions.

sentences.zh: 故天下家給人足，頌聲並作。

translations.literal: Therefore all under Heaven households supplied and men sufficient, eulogizing voices arose together.
translations.idiomatic: The realm then knew sufficiency in every home, and praise for the throne was universal.

sentences.zh: 至高祖、孝文、孝景皇帝，循古節儉，宮女不過十餘，廄馬百餘匹。

translations.literal: Reaching Gaozu, Emperor Wen, and Emperor Jing, they followed antiquity in restraint; palace women did not exceed ten-odd, stable horses a hundred-odd pairs.
translations.idiomatic: Down to Gaozu and Emperors Wen and Jing the court still observed those limits—barely a dozen concubines and little more than a hundred stable horses.

sentences.zh: 孝文皇帝衣綈履革，器亡琱文金銀之飾。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen's garments were pongee and shoes leather; implements had no carved patterns or gold and silver ornament.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen wore homespun silk and leather shoes, and his utensils bore neither chasing nor gold and silver trim.

sentences.zh: 後世爭為奢侈，轉轉益盛，臣下亦相放效，衣服履恊刀劍亂於主上，主上時臨朝入廟，眾人不能別異，甚非其宜。

translations.literal: Later generations strove to be extravagant, turning and turning ever more flourishing; ministers and subordinates also mutually imitated; clothes, shoes, girdles, knives, and swords were disorderly relative to the lord above; when the lord above from time to time attended court and entered the temple, the host of people could not distinguish the difference—very much not appropriate.
translations.idiomatic: Later ages competed in luxury until officials dressed and armed themselves like the sovereign; when the emperor appeared at court or in the ancestral shrine, subjects could scarcely be told apart from their ruler—a scandalous confusion of rank.

sentences.zh: 然非自知奢僭也，猶魯昭公曰：「吾何僭矣？」

translations.literal: Yet it is not that they themselves knew extravagance and usurpation; still like Duke Zhao of Lu said, "In what have I usurped?"
translations.idiomatic: They did not even see their own presumption, any more than Duke Zhao of Lu when he asked, "Where have I overstepped?"

sentences.zh: 今大夫僭諸侯，諸侯僭天子，天子過天道，其日久矣。

translations.literal: Now grandees usurp the feudal lords, the feudal lords usurp the Son of Heaven, the Son of Heaven exceeds the Way of Heaven—the time has been long.
translations.idiomatic: Today ministers ape kings, kings ape the emperor, and the emperor outruns Heaven itself—and the habit is old.

sentences.zh: 承衰救亂，矯復古化，在於陛下。

translations.literal: Receiving decline and saving disorder, correcting and returning to ancient transformation, lies in Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: To heal a declining age and set things right, the power lies with you alone.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為盡如太古難，宜少放古以自節焉。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks completely like high antiquity is difficult; it is fitting slightly to imitate antiquity in order to restrain oneself.
translations.idiomatic: I do not ask you to revive the high archaic age overnight, but you can still borrow its restraint to curb the present excess.

sentences.zh: 論語曰：「君子樂節禮樂。」

translations.literal: The Analects says: "The gentleman delights in restraining ritual and music."
translations.idiomatic: The Analects says the gentleman "finds joy in restraining himself with ritual and music."

sentences.zh: 方今宮室已定，亡可奈何矣，其餘盡可減損。

translations.literal: At present palaces and chambers are already settled; there is nothing to be done about it; the rest all can be reduced and diminished.
translations.idiomatic: The palace complexes are already built and cannot be undone, but everything else can still be cut back.

sentences.zh: 故時齊三服官輸物不過十笥，方今齊三服官作工各數千人，一歲費數鉅萬。

translations.literal: In former times the Qi three-garment office sent goods not exceeding ten cases; now the Qi three-garment office workmen each number several thousand persons, one year's expense several tens of millions.
translations.idiomatic: The Qi workshops that once supplied three grades of imperial garments shipped no more than ten chests a year; today they employ thousands of artisans and cost many millions annually.

sentences.zh: 蜀廣漢主金銀器，歲各用五百萬。

translations.literal: Shu and Guanghan supervised gold and silver vessels; each year each used five million.
translations.idiomatic: The Shu and Guanghan agencies for gold and silver plate each burn through five million cash a year.

sentences.zh: 三工官官費五千萬，東西織室亦然。

translations.literal: The three craft offices' official expense is fifty million; east and west weaving chambers are likewise.
translations.idiomatic: The three imperial craft bureaus spend fifty million; the eastern and western weaving shops cost as much.

sentences.zh: 廄馬食粟將萬匹。

translations.literal: Stable horses eating grain will be ten thousand pairs.
translations.idiomatic: The stables now hold nearly ten thousand grain-fed horses.

sentences.zh: 臣禹嘗從之東宮，見賜杯案，盡文畫金銀飾，非當所以賜食臣下也。

translations.literal: Your servant Yu once followed to the Eastern Palace, saw bestowed cups and tables, all patterned and painted with gold and silver ornament—not what is fitting for bestowing food to ministers below.
translations.idiomatic: I once attended in the heir apparent's eastern palace and saw cups and trays so encrusted with gold and silver chasing that they were unfit gifts even for high ministers.

sentences.zh: 東宮之費亦不可勝計。

translations.literal: The expense of the Eastern Palace also cannot be fully calculated.
translations.idiomatic: The heir apparent's establishment spends sums beyond reckoning.

sentences.zh: 天下之民所為大飢餓死者，是也。

translations.literal: That which the people of all under Heaven do as great hunger and dying—this is it.
translations.idiomatic: This is why the common people starve and die in heaps.

sentences.zh: 今民大飢而死，死又不葬，為犬豬所食。

translations.literal: Now the people greatly hunger and die; dying they are again not buried, and are eaten by dogs and pigs.
translations.idiomatic: They drop from famine in the roads, and corpses lie unburied until dogs and swine tear them apart.

sentences.zh: 人至相食，而廄馬食粟，苦其大肥，氣盛怒至，乃日步作之。

translations.literal: Men reach to mutually eating, yet stable horses eat grain; they suffer their being too fat, vapor flourishes and anger arrives, then daily they are walked to make them.
translations.idiomatic: While men resort to cannibalism, the imperial horses gorge on grain until grooms must walk them daily to work off their fat.

sentences.zh: 王者受命於天，為民父母，固當若此乎！

translations.literal: The king who possesses the state receives mandate from Heaven, acts as father and mother to the people—solidly ought it to be like this!
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven is parent to the people by Heaven's charge—can this be what such a parent should allow?

sentences.zh: 天不見邪？

translations.literal: Does Heaven not see the wrong?
translations.idiomatic: Does Heaven not see this wrong?

sentences.zh: 武帝時，又多取好女至數千人，以填後宮。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Wu, again many took good women reaching several thousand persons, to fill the rear palace.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu drafted thousands of attractive women until the rear palace was packed.

sentences.zh: 及棄天下，昭帝幼弱，霍光專事，不知禮正，妄多臧金錢財物，鳥獸魚鱉牛馬虎豹生禽，凡百九十物，盡瘞臧之，又皆以後宮女置於園陵，大失禮，逆天心，又未必稱武帝意也。

translations.literal: When he abandoned all under Heaven, Emperor Zhao was young and weak; Huo Guang monopolized affairs, did not know ritual correctness, recklessly stored much gold, money, and goods, birds, beasts, fish, turtles, oxen, horses, tigers, leopards, living creatures—in all one hundred ninety things, all buried and stored; again all used rear palace women placed at the garden tombs—greatly losing ritual, contrary to Heaven's mind, and again not necessarily matching Emperor Wu's intention.
translations.idiomatic: When he died, the boy Emperor Zhao left Huo Guang in charge; ignorant of proper burial rite, Guang buried treasure by the cartload—live birds, beasts, fish, cattle, horses, even tigers and leopards, nearly two hundred kinds of creature—and sealed palace women in the tomb precincts. That violated Heaven as well as the late emperor's true wishes.

sentences.zh: 昭帝晏駕，光復行之。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhao's late carriage; Guang again did it.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhao in turn died, Huo Guang repeated the same abuses.

sentences.zh: 至孝宣皇帝時，陛下烏有所言，群臣亦隨故事，甚可痛也！

translations.literal: Reaching Emperor Xuan's time, how could Your Majesty have had words; the host of ministers also followed old precedents—very much to be lamented!
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan you yourself, though heir, had no chance to remonstrate; your ministers likewise clung to precedent—and the wrong has never been redressed.

sentences.zh: 故使天下承化，取女皆大過度，諸侯妻妾或至數百人，豪富吏民畜歌者至數十人，是以內多怨女，外多曠夫。

translations.literal: Therefore causing all under Heaven to receive transformation, taking wives all greatly exceeded measure; feudal lords' wives and concubines sometimes reached several hundred persons; powerful rich clerks and commoners who kept singers reached several tens of persons; therefore within there were many resentful women, without many husbandless men.
translations.idiomatic: The court's example taught the realm to overfill harems: some lords kept hundreds of concubines, rich households dozens of singing girls, while unmarried women languished inside palace walls and unmarried men wandered outside.

sentences.zh: 及眾庶葬埋，皆虛地上以實地下。

translations.literal: Reaching the host of commoners' burying, all emptied what was above ground to fill what was below ground.
translations.idiomatic: Commoners likewise beggared their living kin to pile treasure under the earth.

sentences.zh: 其過自上生，皆在大臣循故事之罪也。

translations.literal: Their excess sprang from what was above produced; all lay in the crime of great ministers following old precedents.
translations.idiomatic: The evil began at the top; the fault lies with ministers who hide behind "that is how it was done before."

sentences.zh: 唯陛下深察古道，從其儉者，大減損乘輿服御器物，三分去二。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty deeply examine the ancient way, follow its frugal ones, greatly reduce and diminish the imperial equipage, garments, and utensils—remove two-thirds of three parts.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to study the frugal models of antiquity and cut the imperial wardrobe, chariots, and furnishings by two-thirds.

sentences.zh: 子產多少有命，審察後宮，擇其賢者留二十人，餘悉歸之。

translations.literal: The rear-palace women, whether many or few, have Heaven-allotted lot; examine the rear palace, select the worthy and keep twenty persons, the rest all return them.
translations.idiomatic: Audit the inner palace: keep twenty ladies of proven character and send the rest home to their families, for no ruler needs a harem beyond Heaven's measure.

sentences.zh: 及諸陵園女亡子者，宜悉遣。

translations.literal: Reaching the garden tomb women without sons, it is fitting entirely to send them.
translations.idiomatic: Dismiss as well the childless attendants still quartered at the imperial tombs.

sentences.zh: 獨杜陵宮人數百，誠可哀憐也。

translations.literal: Only the Duling palace persons number several hundred—truly pitiable.
translations.idiomatic: Hundreds of women still languish at the Duling shrine alone—surely a sight to pity.

sentences.zh: 廄馬可亡過數十匹。

translations.literal: Stable horses can be without exceeding several tens of pairs.
translations.idiomatic: Cut the stable to a few dozen grain-fed horses.

sentences.zh: 獨舍長安城南苑地以為田獵之囿，自城西南至山西至鄠皆復其田，以與貧民。

translations.literal: Only leave the southern preserve land of Chang'an as field and hunting preserve; from the city southwest to the mountains west to Hu all restore their fields, to give to the poor people.
translations.idiomatic: Reserve only the southern hunting park at Chang'an; return the rest of the capital preserves—from the southwest wall to the western hills and Hu county—to tillage and give the plots to the destitute.

sentences.zh: 方今天下飢饉，可亡大自損減以救之，稱天意乎？

translations.literal: At present all under Heaven is famine and hunger; can there be without greatly self-reducing and diminishing to save it, matching Heaven's intention?
translations.idiomatic: The realm is starving; will you not slash court spending yourself to answer Heaven's will?

sentences.zh: 天生聖人，蓋為萬民，非獨使自娛樂而已也。

translations.literal: Heaven born the sage man, probably is for the myriad people; not alone causing him to amuse and pleasure himself.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven appoints a sage for the myriad lives under him, not so he may amuse himself.

sentences.zh: 故《詩》曰：「天難諶斯，不易惟王；」

translations.literal: Therefore the Odes says: "Heaven is hard to trust; it is not easy to be king;"
translations.idiomatic: The Odes warn, "Heaven's mandate is hard to read; the king's task is never easy;"

sentences.zh: 「上帝臨女，毋貳爾心。」

translations.literal: "The High Lord looks down on you; do not be double in your heart."
translations.idiomatic: "High God watches you—do not harbor a divided heart."

sentences.zh: 「當仁不讓」，獨可以聖心參諸天地，揆之往古，不可與臣下議也。

translations.literal: "When it is benevolent, do not yield"—only can the sage mind consult Heaven and earth, measure against past antiquity; cannot be discussed with ministers below.
translations.idiomatic: The maxim "do not yield the ground of humanity" means you must weigh such reforms in your own sage mind against Heaven and history—not dilute them in routine debate with ministers.

sentences.zh: 若其阿意順指，隨君上下，臣禹不勝拳拳，不敢不盡愚心。

translations.literal: If it is flattering intent and following finger, following the lord up and down, your servant Yu cannot overcome earnest loyalty, does not dare not exhaust foolish mind.
translations.idiomatic: If you prefer flattery, I cannot force you; yet I would fail my duty if I did not speak plainly.

sentences.zh: 天子納善其忠，乃下詔令太僕減食穀馬，水衡減食肉獸，省宜春下苑以與貧民，又罷角抵諸戲及齊三服官。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven received as good his loyalty, then issued an edict ordering the Grand Coachman to reduce grain-eating horses, the Superintendent of Waters to reduce meat-eating beasts, saved Yichun lower preserve to give to the poor people, and again abolished horn butting and various games and the Qi three-garment office.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor welcomed his honesty and ordered the grand coachman to cut grain-fed horses, the superintendent of waters to cut meat-fed palace beasts, turned over the lower Yichun park to the poor, and ended wrestling shows and the Qi garment workshops.

sentences.zh: 遷禹為光祿大夫。

translations.literal: Yu was transferred to be Grand Counselor of the Household.
translations.idiomatic: Gong Yu was promoted to grand counselor of the household.

sentences.zh: 頃之，禹上書曰：「臣禹年老貧窮，家訾不滿萬錢，妻子㐄豆不贍，裋褐不完。

translations.literal: Shortly, Yu submitted a memorial saying: "Your servant Yu is old in years and poor, household property not full ten thousand cash, wife and children coarse beans not sufficient, short coat not complete.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward Gong Yu wrote again: "I am old and destitute—family assets under ten thousand cash, wife and children without enough coarse grain to fill a bowl, and not even a whole homespun coat between us.

sentences.zh: 有田百三十畝，陛下過意徵臣，臣賣田百畝以供車馬。

translations.literal: There are fields one hundred thirty mu; Your Majesty passing intention summoned your servant; your servant sold fields one hundred mu in order to supply chariots and horses.
translations.idiomatic: I owned only one hundred thirty mu of land; when Your Majesty graciously summoned me, I sold a hundred mu to pay for the journey to the capital.

sentences.zh: 至，拜為諫大夫，秩八百石，奉錢月九千二百。

translations.literal: When arrived, was appointed remonstrance grandee, rank eight hundred shi, salary cash monthly nine thousand two hundred.
translations.idiomatic: On arrival I was named remonstrance grandee at eight hundred shi—nine thousand two hundred cash a month.

sentences.zh: 廩食太官，又蒙賞賜四時雜繒綿絮衣服酒肉諸果物，德厚甚深。

translations.literal: Granary food from the Grand Provisioner, again received bestowals of the four seasons miscellaneous silks, floss, clothes, wine, meat, various fruits and things—virtue thick and very deep.
translations.idiomatic: The court fed me from the imperial kitchen and showered me with seasonal gifts—silks, padding, robes, wine, meat, and fruit—kindness beyond measure.

sentences.zh: 疾病侍醫臨治，賴陛下神靈，不死而活。

translations.literal: In illness attending physicians came to treat; relying on Your Majesty's numinous power, not dying but living.
translations.idiomatic: When I fell ill, palace physicians attended me; by your grace I survived what might have killed me.

sentences.zh: 又拜為光祿大夫，秩二千石，奉錢月萬二千。

translations.literal: Again was appointed Grand Counselor of the Household, rank two thousand shi, salary cash monthly twelve thousand.
translations.idiomatic: I was then promoted to grand counselor of the household at two thousand shi—twelve thousand cash each month.

sentences.zh: 祿賜愈多，家日以益富，身日以益尊，誠非屮茅愚臣所當蒙也。

translations.literal: Salary and bestowals ever more many, the household daily thereby became richer, the person daily thereby became more honored—truly not what a grass-hut foolish minister ought to receive.
translations.idiomatic: Stipends and gifts multiplied until my family grew wealthy and my person exalted—honors no humble subject should ever expect.

sentences.zh: 伏自念終亡以報厚恩，日夜慚愧而已。

translations.literal: Prostrate, I think to the end there is no means to repay the thick favor; day and night shame and guilt only.
translations.idiomatic: I know I can never repay such bounty; shame is all I feel, waking and sleeping.

sentences.zh: 臣禹犬馬之齒八十一，血氣衰竭，耳目不聰明，非復能有補益，所謂素餐尸祿洿朝之臣也。

translations.literal: Your servant Yu's dog-and-horse teeth are eighty-one; blood and vapor fail and exhaust, ears and eyes are not sharp and bright; no longer able to have benefit, what is called eating plain food and occupying salary, defiling the court minister.
translations.idiomatic: I am eighty-one years old, my strength spent and my senses dull; I can no longer serve usefully—I am the sort who "eats free rice" and disgraces the court.

sentences.zh: 自痛去家三千里，凡有一子，年十二，非有在家為臣具棺槨者也。

translations.literal: Self-pained at leaving home three thousand li; in all there is one son, age twelve; there is none at home who as minister prepares coffin and outer shell.
translations.idiomatic: I am three thousand li from home with only a twelve-year-old son and no kin there to arrange a decent burial should I die.

sentences.zh: 誠恐一旦蹎仆氣竭，不復自還，洿席薦於宮室，骸骨棄捐，孤魂不歸。

translations.literal: Truly fearing that in one morning stumbling and falling, vapor exhausted, not again returning by myself, defiling mats and cushions in the palace chambers, bones cast aside, lone soul not returning.
translations.idiomatic: I dread collapsing one morning on your palace floor—unable to journey home, my corpse fouling your halls and my ghost stranded far from kin.

sentences.zh: 不勝私願，願乞骸骨，及身生歸鄉里，死亡所恨。」

translations.literal: Unable to overcome private wish; I wish to beg bones, while alive return to village and hamlet—death is what I would regret."
translations.idiomatic: I beg leave to surrender my bones: let me go home alive while I still can, for that alone would leave me without regret."

sentences.zh: 天子報曰：「朕以生有伯夷之廉，史魚之直，守經據古，不阿當世，孳孳於民，俗之所寡，故親近生，幾參國政。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven replied saying: "I because you have Boyi's incorruptibility, Shi Yu's uprightness, guard the classics and rely on antiquity, do not flatter the present age, diligently work for the people—what custom lacks—therefore I draw near you, almost share state government.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered: "I drew you near because you combine Boyi's purity with Shi Yu's blunt honesty—you hold to the classics, scorn fashion, and labor for the people where others fall short. I meant for you to share in governing.

sentences.zh: 今未得久聞生之奇論也，而云欲退，意豈有所恨與？

translations.literal: Now I have not been able long to hear your strange discussions, yet you say you wish to retire—is the intention perhaps that you harbor some resentment?"
translations.idiomatic: I have not yet heard enough of your uncommon counsel, yet you speak of retiring—do you nurse some grievance against me?"

sentences.zh: 將在位者與生殊乎？

translations.literal: Is it that those in office are at odds with you?"
translations.idiomatic: Or is it that you feel slighted by the ministers already in power?"

sentences.zh: 往者嘗令金敞語生，欲及生時祿生之子，既已諭矣，今復云子少。

translations.literal: In the past I once ordered Jin Chang to speak to you, wishing while you lived to salary your son; already it was announced; now again you say the son is young.
translations.idiomatic: I already told Jin Chang to see that your son received a stipend while you live; you now plead that he is too young.

sentences.zh: 夫以王命辨護生家，雖百子何以加？

translations.literal: As to using kingly command to discriminate and protect your household—even a hundred sons, how could anything be added?
translations.idiomatic: With the throne watching over your household, what more could a hundred sons require?
