translations.literal: Within the seas tribute and presentation ought to nurture one lord; now on the contrary it is all exhausted to Xian's household—is this Heaven's intent and the people's intent!
translations.idiomatic: The tribute of the empire should feed one sovereign—yet it all flows to Dong Xian's door. Can that be Heaven's will or the people's?

sentences.zh: 天下可久負，厚之如此，反所以害之也。

translations.literal: All under Heaven can long be burdened; thickening him like this on the contrary is the means to harm him.
translations.idiomatic: The realm cannot bear such favor forever; to spoil him so is to doom him.

sentences.zh: 誠欲哀賢，宜為謝過天地，解讎海內，免遣就國，收乘輿器物，還之縣官。

translations.literal: If truly wishing to pity Xian, it is fitting to apologize fault to Heaven and Earth, release enmity within the seas, dismiss and send to his state, collect imperial equipage and objects, return them to the county office.
translations.idiomatic: If you pity him, confess the fault before Heaven and earth, appease the empire's anger, send him back to his fief, and confiscate every piece of imperial gear he has taken.

sentences.zh: 如此，可以父子終其性命；

translations.literal: Like this, father and son can finish their life spans;
translations.idiomatic: Then father and son may live out their natural years;

sentences.zh: 不者，海內之所讎，未有得久安者也。

translations.literal: if not, what all within the seas hates—none have been able long to be secure.
translations.idiomatic: otherwise the hatred of the realm will swallow them—no favorite has ever lasted long on that tide.

sentences.zh: 孫寵、息夫躬不宜居國，可皆免以視天下。

translations.literal: Sun Chong and Xifu Gong are not fitting to dwell in their states; all can be dismissed thereby to show all under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Chong and Xifu Gong should not be left in their fiefs—strip them entirely as a signal to the realm.

sentences.zh: 復徵何武、師丹、彭宣、傅喜，曠然使民易視，以應天心，建立大政，以興太平之端。

translations.literal: Again summon He Wu, Shi Dan, Peng Xuan, and Fu Xi—openly causing the people to change their view, thereby to answer Heaven's mind, establish great government, and raise the beginning of great peace.
translations.idiomatic: Recall He Wu, Shi Dan, Peng Xuan, and Fu Xi—give the people something new to look up to, answer Heaven, and lay the foundation of true peace.

sentences.zh: 高門去省戶數十步，求見出入，二年未省，欲使海瀕仄陋自通，遠矣！

translations.literal: The high gate is removed from the provincial door by several tens of steps; seeking audience going in and out, two years not received—wishing to make sea-shore narrow and lowly self connect is far!
translations.idiomatic: The palace gate lies only a few dozen paces from the censor's yamen, yet for two years I could not win an audience—how much farther off are the humble petitioners of the coast!

sentences.zh: 願賜數刻之間，極竭毣毣之思，退入三泉，死亡所恨。

translations.literal: I wish to be granted several notches of time, exhaust utterly the anxious thoughts, retire and enter the three springs—death is what I would regret.
translations.idiomatic: Grant me a few moments to pour out every scruple; then let me sink into the three springs of the dead—I shall die without regret.

sentences.zh: 上感大異，納宣言，徵何武、彭宣，旬月皆復為三公。

translations.literal: The emperor was moved by the great portents, accepted Xuan's words, summoned He Wu and Peng Xuan; within a ten-day month both again became the Three Dukes.
translations.idiomatic: Moved by the omens, the emperor took his advice and recalled He Wu and Peng Xuan; within the month both were back among the Three Dukes.

sentences.zh: 拜宣為司隸。

translations.literal: Xuan was appointed Metropolitan Superintendent.
translations.idiomatic: Bao Xuan was named metropolitan superintendent.

sentences.zh: 時哀帝改司隸校尉但為司隸，官比司直。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor Ai changed Metropolitan Superintendent (sili xiaowei) to only Sili, the office rank comparable to Director of Integrity (sizhi).
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ai had dropped "colonel" from the title, leaving the post simply "metropolitan superintendent," with rank on a par with the chancellor's director of integrity.

sentences.zh: 丞相孔光四時行園陵，官屬以令行馳道中，宣出逢之，使吏鉤止丞相掾史，沒入其車馬，摧辱宰相。

translations.literal: Chancellor Kong Guang in the four seasons went to the garden tombs; subordinates by order went on the expressway; Xuan went out and met them, ordered clerks to hook and stop the chancellor's aides and clerks, confiscated their chariots and horses, crushed and insulted the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: When Chancellor Kong Guang toured the imperial tombs, his staff rode the imperial carriage-way as regulations allowed; Bao Xuan intercepted them, had the aides arrested, impounded their horses, and publicly humiliated the chief minister.

sentences.zh: 事下御史，中丞侍御史至司隸官，欲捕從事，閉門不肯內。

translations.literal: The affair was sent down to the imperial secretary; the assistant and attending imperial secretary came to the metropolitan superintendent's office, wished to arrest the staff aide; closed the door and was not willing to admit.
translations.idiomatic: The case went to the imperial secretariat; when its officers came to arrest his aide, Bao Xuan locked his gate and refused them entry.

sentences.zh: 宣坐距閉使者，亡人臣禮，大不敬，不道，下廷尉獄。

translations.literal: Xuan sat for resisting and closing the envoy, lacking the rite of a minister, greatly disrespectful, not the Way; was sent down to the commandant of justice prison.
translations.idiomatic: He was charged with blocking imperial officers, gross disrespect, and conduct unworthy of a minister, and jailed pending execution.

sentences.zh: 博士弟子濟南王咸舉幡太學下，曰：「欲救鮑司隸者會此下。」

translations.literal: An erudite's disciple Wang Xian of Ji'nan raised a banner below the Imperial Academy, saying: "Those who wish to save Metropolitan Bao assemble here below."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xian of Ji'nan, a student at the Imperial Academy, hoisted a banner: "Who would save Metropolitan Bao, gather here."

sentences.zh: 諸生會者千餘人。

translations.literal: The students who assembled were more than a thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: More than a thousand students rallied.

sentences.zh: 朝日，遮丞相孔光自言，丞相車不得行，又守闕上書。

translations.literal: At court time they blocked Chancellor Kong Guang and spoke for themselves; the chancellor's carriage could not proceed; again guarded the gate and submitted memorials.
translations.idiomatic: At dawn they barred Chancellor Kong Guang's carriage and pleaded; they also besieged the palace gate with petitions.

sentences.zh: 上遂抵宣罪減死一等，髡鉗。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon reduced Xuan's crime one grade from death, shaved and manacled.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor commuted the death sentence to shaving and the cangue.

sentences.zh: 宣既被刑，乃徙之上黨，以為其地宜田牧，又少豪俊，易長雄，遂家于長子。

translations.literal: Xuan having received punishment, then was moved to Shangdang, thinking that its land was fitting for fields and pasture, and again had few heroic outstanding men, easy to grow and excel, thereupon made his home in Changzi.
translations.idiomatic: After his sentence he was banished to Shangdang, where land was good for farming and herding and local bullies were few; he settled in Changzi county.

sentences.zh: 平帝即位，王莽秉政，陰有篡國之心，乃風州郡以罪法案誅諸豪桀，及漢忠直臣不附己者，宣及何武等皆死。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ping ascended the throne, Wang Mang held power, inwardly had the mind to usurp the state, then instructed commanderies and states by crime cases to execute various heroic outstanding men, and Han loyal and upright ministers who did not attach to himself—Xuan and He Wu and others all died.
translations.idiomatic: When the boy Emperor Ping came to the throne, Wang Mang began plotting usurpation and hinted to the provinces to frame and execute local strongmen and Han loyalists who would not bend—Bao Xuan and He Wu were among those killed.

sentences.zh: 時名捕隴西辛興，興與宣女婿許紺俱過宣，一飯去，宣不知情，坐繫獄，自殺。

translations.literal: At that time there was a famous warrant for Xin Xing of Longxi; Xing together with Xuan's son-in-law Xu Dan both passed Xuan, one meal and departed; Xuan did not know the circumstance, sat and was bound in prison, killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: When Longxi issued a warrant for Xin Xing, he stopped once at Bao Xuan's house with Xu Dan, Bao's son-in-law, shared a meal, and left; Bao Xuan knew nothing of the charge, was arrested as an accomplice, and took his own life in prison.

sentences.zh: 自成帝至王莽時，清名之士，琅邪又有紀逡王思，齊則薛方子容，太原則郇越臣仲、郇相稚賓，沛郡則唐林子高、唐尊伯高，皆以明經飭行顯名於世。

translations.literal: From the time of Emperor Cheng down to Wang Mang's time, among men of pure reputation Langya again had Ji Qun and Wang Si, Qi then Xue Fang style Zirong, Taiyuan then Xun Yue style Zhongzhong, Xun Xiang style Zhibin, Pei commandery then Tang Lin style Zigao, Tang Zun style Bogao—all by illumined classics and polished conduct displayed name in the age.
translations.idiomatic: From Emperor Cheng to Wang Mang, noted men of integrity included Ji Qun and Wang Si of Langya, Xue Fang (Zirong) of Qi, Xun Yue and Xun Xiang of Taiyuan, and Tang Lin and Tang Zun of Pei—all famed for classical learning and upright lives.

sentences.zh: 紀逡、兩唐皆仕王莽，封侯貴重，歷公卿位。

translations.literal: Ji Qun and the two Tangs all served Wang Mang, were enfeoffed marquises, noble and heavy, passed through duke and minister rank.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Qun and both Tangs served Wang Mang, were ennobled as marquises, and rose to the highest offices.

sentences.zh: 唐林數上疏諫正，有忠直節。

translations.literal: Tang Lin several times submitted memorials remonstrating and correcting, had loyal and upright integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Tang Lin repeatedly remonstrated with blunt honesty.

sentences.zh: 唐尊衣敝履空，以瓦器飲食，又以歷遺公卿，被虛偽名。

translations.literal: Tang Zun wore tattered clothes and empty shoes, used tile vessels for drink and food, again used a calendar to present to dukes and ministers—bore a false reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Tang Zun affected rags, holey shoes, meals from earthenware, and calendars handed out to ministers—a hollow show of austerity.

sentences.zh: 郇越、相，同族昆弟也，並舉州郡孝廉茂材，數病，去官。

translations.literal: Xun Yue and Xiang, same clan cousins, together were raised commandery filial and incorrupt and abundant talent, several times by illness left office.
translations.idiomatic: The cousins Xun Yue and Xun Xiang were both recommended as filial, incorrupt, and abundant talent, but ill health repeatedly drove them from office.

sentences.zh: 越散其先人訾千餘萬，以分施九族州里，志節尤高。

translations.literal: Yue scattered his ancestors' property more than ten million, thereby to divide and bestow on nine agnates and village and hamlet—purpose and integrity especially high.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Yue gave away over ten million cash of inherited wealth to kin and neighbors—his purpose was of the highest.

sentences.zh: 相王莽時徵為太子四友，病死，莽太子遣使裞以衣衾，其子攀棺不聽，曰：「死父遺言，師友之送勿有所受，今於皇太子得託友官，故不受也。」

translations.literal: Xiang in Wang Mang's time was summoned to be one of the heir apparent's four friends; by illness died; Mang's heir apparent sent an envoy to condole with clothes and coverlet; his son climbed the coffin and would not listen, saying: "Dead father's dying words: from tutor and friends' sending do not accept anything; now with the imperial heir apparent having been able to entrust the office of friend, therefore do not accept."
translations.idiomatic: Xun Xiang was summoned as one of the heir apparent's four friends under Wang Mang but died in office; the heir sent mourning gifts, yet Xun's son clung to the coffin and refused them, saying, "My father's last charge was to accept nothing from tutor or friend; though the heir calls him friend, we will not take these."

sentences.zh: 京師稱之。

translations.literal: The capital praised this.
translations.idiomatic: The capital praised his resolve.

sentences.zh: 薛方嘗為郡掾祭酒，嘗徵不至，及莽以安車迎方，方因使者辭謝曰：「堯舜在上，下有巢由，今明主方隆唐虞之德，小臣欲守箕山之節也。」

translations.literal: Xue Fang once was commandery clerk libationer; once was summoned and did not arrive; when Mang with comfortable carriage welcomed Fang, Fang through the envoy declined with thanks saying: "Yao and Shun above, below there were Chao and You; now the bright lord is just raising Tang and Yu's virtue; your petty minister wishes to keep the integrity of Mount Ji."
translations.idiomatic: Xue Fang had been a commandery libationer and once ignored a summons; when Wang Mang sent a carriage for him, he told the envoy, "Under Yao and Shun there were still hermits like Chao and Xu; Your Majesty revives their virtue, while I prefer the reclusion of Mount Ji."

sentences.zh: 使者以聞，莽說其言，不強致。

translations.literal: The envoy reported it upward; Mang was pleased with his words, did not force to bring him in.
translations.idiomatic: The envoy reported his words; Wang Mang was pleased and did not press him.

sentences.zh: 方居家以經教授，喜屬文，著詩賦數十篇。

translations.literal: Fang dwelt at home and by the classics taught, loved composing texts, composed odes and rhapsodies several tens of pieces.
translations.idiomatic: Xue Fang taught the classics at home, loved letters, and left several dozen poems and fu.

sentences.zh: 始隃麋郭欽，哀帝時為丞相司直，奏免豫州牧鮑宣、京兆尹薛修等，又奏董賢，左遷盧奴令，平帝時遷南郡太守。

translations.literal: At first Guo Qin of Yumi, in Emperor Ai's time was Director of Integrity for the Chancellor, submitted memorials dismissing Governor of Yu Province Bao Xuan, Metropolitan Governor Xue Xiu and others, again submitted against Dong Xian, was transferred left to magistrate of Lunu, in Emperor Ping's time was transferred to Governor of Nan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Qin of Yumi had been the chancellor's director of integrity under Emperor Ai who impeached Bao Xuan and Xue Xiu and also struck at Dong Xian, was demoted to magistrate of Lunu, and under Emperor Ping became governor of Nan commandery.

sentences.zh: 而杜陵蔣詡元卿為兗州刺史，亦以廉直為名。

translations.literal: And Jiang Xu style Yuanqing of Duling was Governor of Yan Province, also by pure uprightness had a name.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Xu of Duling, as governor of Yan Province, was likewise famed for honesty.

sentences.zh: 王莽居攝，欽、詡皆以病免官，歸鄉里，臥不出戶，卒於家。

translations.literal: Wang Mang as regent, Qin and Xu both by illness left office, returned to village and hamlet, lay and did not go out the door, died at home.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang became regent, both Guo Qin and Jiang Xu pleaded illness, retired to their villages, never crossed their thresholds again, and died at home.

sentences.zh: 齊栗融客卿、北海禽慶子夏、蘇章游卿、山陽曹竟子期皆儒生，去官不仕於莽。

translations.literal: Qi's Li Rong style Keqing, Beihai's Qin Qing style Zixia, Su Zhang style Youqing, Shanyang's Cao Jing style Ziqi were all Confucian students; left office and did not serve under Mang.
translations.idiomatic: Li Rong of Qi, Qin Qing of Beihai, Su Zhang, and Cao Jing of Shanyang were scholars who quit office rather than serve Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 莽死，漢更始徵竟以為丞相，封侯，欲視致賢人，銷寇賊。

translations.literal: When Mang died, Han Gengshi summoned Jing to be chancellor, enfeoffed marquis, wished to show bringing worthies and melt away bandits.
translations.idiomatic: After Wang Mang fell, Emperor Gengshi of Han summoned Cao Jing as chancellor and offered a marquisate to display his zeal for worthies and to disarm the rebels.

sentences.zh: 竟不受侯爵。

translations.literal: Jing did not accept the marquis' noble rank.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Jing refused the marquisate.

sentences.zh: 會赤眉入長安，欲降竟，竟手劍格死。

translations.literal: It happened the Red Eyebrows entered Chang'an, wished to make Jing submit; Jing with hand sword resisted and died.
translations.idiomatic: When the Red Eyebrows took Chang'an they tried to force him to yield; he crossed swords with them and died fighting.

sentences.zh: 世祖即位，徵薛方，道病卒。

translations.literal: When the Founding Ancestor ascended the throne, summoned Xue Fang, on the road by illness died.
translations.idiomatic: When Guangwu came to the throne he summoned Xue Fang, who died en route.

sentences.zh: 兩龔、鮑宣子孫皆見褒表，至大官。

translations.literal: The two Gongs' and Bao Xuan's sons and grandsons all were praised and marked, reaching high office.
translations.idiomatic: The descendants of the two Gongs and Bao Xuan were honored and rose to high rank.

translations.literal: 【Appraisal】
translations.idiomatic: Appraisal

sentences.zh: 贊曰：易稱「君子之道，或出或處，或默或語」，言其各得道之一節，譬諸草木，區以別矣。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: The Changes calls "the gentleman's way—sometimes going out, sometimes dwelling in, sometimes silent, sometimes speaking"—speaking of each obtaining one joint of the Way; compared to grass and trees, they are divided and distinguished.
translations.idiomatic: The appraisal: The Book of Changes says the gentleman "may serve or withdraw, speak or hold silence"—each path catches one facet of the Way, as plants differ in kind.

sentences.zh: 故曰山林之士往而不能反，朝廷之士入而不能出，二者各有所短。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: men of mountain and forest go but cannot return; men of the court enter but cannot come out—the two each have what they lack.
translations.idiomatic: Recluses may leave the world but cannot easily return; courtiers may enter power but cannot easily leave—each path has its cost.

sentences.zh: 春秋列國卿大夫至漢興將相名臣，懷祿耽寵以失其世者多矣！

translations.literal: From the Spring and Autumn Annals' feudal states' ministers and grandees down to Han's rise, generals and chancellors and famed ministers—those who cherished salary and doted on favor and thereby lost their generation are very many!
translations.idiomatic: From the feudal age to Han, countless generals and ministers clung to stipend and favor until they ruined themselves and their times.

sentences.zh: 是故清節之士於是為貴。

translations.literal: Therefore men of pure integrity were prized in this.
translations.idiomatic: Hence men of stainless principle won the highest esteem.

sentences.zh: 然大率多能自治而不能治人。

translations.literal: Yet in general most were able to order themselves but not able to order others.
translations.idiomatic: Yet most could govern their own persons, not the realm.

sentences.zh: 王、貢之材，優於龔、鮑。

translations.literal: In the talent of Wang and Gong, they are superior to Gong and Bao.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ji and Gong Yu had the greater political talent; the two Gongs and Bao Xuan stand more narrowly on moral heroism.

sentences.zh: 守死善道，勝實蹈焉。

translations.literal: Guarding death and loving the Way—Sheng in truth trod it.
translations.idiomatic: To die for the right principle—Gong Sheng truly walked that path.

sentences.zh: 貞而不諒，薛方近之。

translations.literal: Upright but not credulous—Xue Fang neared it.
translations.idiomatic: Upright yet supple in refusal—Xue Fang came close to that balance.

sentences.zh: 郭欽、蔣詡好遯不汙，絕紀、唐矣！

translations.literal: Guo Qin and Jiang Xu loved reclusion and were not defiled—they cut off Ji and Tang!
translations.idiomatic: Guo Qin and Jiang Xu chose reclusion without stain—here was integrity that put Ji Qun and the Tang brothers to shame.
